real_woman.name: Marie Curie
real_woman.achievement: First woman to win a Nobel Prize, and the only person to win Nobel Prizes in two different sciences

story.title: The Girl Who Found the Glow

pages.text: Lily loved science fair day more than almost anything. She loved the bubbling volcanoes and the spinning planets and the jars full of growing crystals. But when she told the boy next to her that she wanted to enter this year, he laughed. "Science is for boys," he said, like it was something everyone already knew. Lily looked around the gymnasium. Most of the projects did have boys standing behind them. Maybe he was right. She put her idea notebook back in her backpack and zipped it shut. On the walk home, the sky was full of clouds, and Lily felt a little like the sun had gone behind one too.
pages.illustration_description: A school gymnasium set up for a science fair. Lily stands near a table, holding a small notebook to her chest, looking uncertain. A boy nearby is gesturing dismissively. Around them are science fair tables with volcanoes, planet models, and crystal jars. The gymnasium is brightly lit with colorful banners, but Lily stands slightly apart from the activity. The mood is warm but tinged with self-doubt. Lily wears a backpack and casual school clothes.

pages.text: That evening, Lily sat on Grandma's porch, picking at a loose thread on her sleeve. "You're quiet tonight," Grandma said, settling into her rocking chair. Lily shrugged. "Someone said science is for boys." Grandma's eyebrows rose so high they almost touched her silver hair. "Is that so?" she said. "Then I think it's time you met someone." She disappeared inside and came back carrying a very old book with a cracked green cover. Gold letters on the front spelled out: REMARKABLE DISCOVERIES. "Open it," Grandma whispered, and her eyes twinkled like she knew a wonderful secret. When Lily opened the cover, the pages began to glow with a soft, pale blue light.
pages.illustration_description: A cozy porch at dusk with a wooden rocking chair where Grandma sits. Lily sits on the porch steps. Grandma is handing Lily an old, weathered book with a cracked green leather cover and gold lettering. The book is cracked open slightly and a mysterious blue-white glow spills out from between the pages, illuminating both their faces with wonder. Fireflies dot the dusky yard behind them. Potted plants and a warm porch light complete the scene. The mood is magical and intimate.

pages.text: The blue glow swirled off the pages like mist, and suddenly Lily could see it — a tiny shed on a cobblestone street in Paris, with rain dripping through cracks in the roof. Inside, a woman with her hair pinned up worked at a long wooden table, surrounded by jars and instruments. A man worked beside her. "That's Marie Curie," Grandma said softly, "and her husband Pierre." In a cold, leaky shed in Paris, Marie and Pierre worked late into the night. The jars on the shelves glowed blue in the dark. Marie had discovered something amazing — a new element that gave off its own light. She called it radium. Lily stared at the glowing jars in the picture, then down at the glow rising from the book. "She found something that made its own light?" Lily breathed. "She found something no one had ever seen before," Grandma said.
pages.illustration_description: A magical double scene: In the foreground, Lily holds the glowing book open on her lap on the porch. Rising from the book's pages like a luminous vision is a detailed scene of Marie Curie's shed laboratory in Paris — a small, humble workspace with a leaky roof, wooden tables covered in scientific instruments, and most importantly, several jars on shelves glowing an ethereal blue-green light. Marie Curie stands at the table in a long dark dress with her hair pinned up, examining a glowing substance. Pierre works beside her. The blue glow connects both scenes — from the jars in the shed to the light spilling from the book. The mood is magical, wondrous, and full of discovery.

pages.text: Grandma turned the glowing page, and the story continued like a fairy tale. Many people told Marie she didn't belong in science. When she was young in Poland, girls weren't even allowed to go to university. So Marie traveled far from home, all the way to Paris, where she studied so hard she sometimes forgot to eat. She worked in that cold little shed for years, stirring huge pots of rock and dust, searching for tiny traces of her glowing element. Marie Curie discovered two new elements and won the Nobel Prize twice — once for physics and once for chemistry. No one else has ever done that. "Not any man or any woman," Grandma said, holding up two fingers. "Two prizes. Two sciences. Only Marie." Lily traced the golden medal drawn on the page and felt something warm bloom in her chest, like a small light turning on.
pages.illustration_description: A richly illustrated storybook page come to life, showing Marie Curie's journey in fairy-tale style: a sequence of vignettes flowing across the page. First, a young Marie reading books by candlelight. Then Marie traveling by train toward a glittering Paris skyline. Then Marie stirring a large pot in her shed, determined and focused. Finally, Marie in a grand hall, holding two golden Nobel Prize medals that radiate light. The images flow together like panels in a magical tapestry, bordered by swirling blue light and golden stars. Grandma points to the medals while Lily looks up with wide, shining eyes. The mood is epic, triumphant, and awe-inspiring.

pages.text: The next morning, Lily unzipped her backpack and pulled out her idea notebook. She opened it to the page where she had sketched her experiment — growing crystals in different liquids to see which one worked best. She added a new title at the top in her best handwriting: "The Crystal Glow Project, by Lily." At school, she marched straight to Ms. Hayward's desk and signed up for the science fair. The boy from the gymnasium looked over. "I thought you weren't entering," he said. "I changed my mind," Lily said, and smiled. "Did you know that Marie Curie won the Nobel Prize twice? She discovered elements that glowed in the dark." The boy blinked. "That's actually really cool," he said. Lily opened her notebook and got to work, and she felt that small light inside her glowing brighter than ever.
pages.illustration_description: A bright, sunlit classroom. Lily stands confidently at the teacher's desk, signing a clipboard for the science fair, her idea notebook open beside her showing colorful sketches of crystals in jars. Her posture is tall and proud. The boy from page one stands nearby, looking genuinely interested and impressed rather than dismissive. Ms. Hayward smiles warmly behind the desk. On the wall behind them is a poster of famous scientists, and among them is a portrait of Marie Curie. Lily's notebook has the title 'The Crystal Glow Project' written in large, careful letters. Sunlight streams through the classroom windows. The mood is hopeful, confident, and joyful — a new beginning.

story.real_woman_achievement: Marie Curie was the first woman to win a Nobel Prize, and she remains the only person in history to win Nobel Prizes in two different sciences — physics and chemistry.
discussion_prompts: Has anyone ever told you that you couldn't do something? How did it make you feel, and what did you do?
Why do you think Marie Curie kept working in that cold, leaky shed even when it was hard?
What is something you're curious about that you'd like to explore or learn more about?
story.activity_suggestion: Grow your own crystals at home! Dissolve sugar or salt in warm water, hang a string in the jar, and watch crystals form over several days. Try different liquids (water, vinegar, juice) to see which grows the best crystals — just like Lily's science fair project!

issues: Page 1 reinforces the stereotype somewhat by showing 'most of the projects did have boys standing behind them' - this validates the boy's claim visually, though it's likely meant to show real-world gender gaps
Minor: The boy's quick acceptance at the end ('That's actually really cool') feels slightly rushed, though not unrealistic for children's content
strengths: Marie Curie is presented with genuine scientific achievement and historical context, not tokenized
Lily is described by her interests and actions, not appearance (no mentions of what she looks like)
The grandmother character provides intergenerational support without being preachy
Marie's struggles are age-appropriate but real (couldn't attend university, worked in poor conditions, faced discrimination)
Ending is empowering but realistic - Lily signs up and gets to work, not instant success
The magical book device makes the historical education feel natural and engaging for the target age
Marie is shown as collaborative (working with Pierre) but clearly credited with her own discoveries
Lily shows agency - she makes her own decision to participate after learning new information
